evidence: FSB thematic review
notes: Regulatory comparison

evidence: Official issuer release summarizing Q1 2026 attestation and reserve allocation

evidence: Official issuer release describing non-Treasury reserve assets

evidence: PayPal terms banner describing transition date and replacement terms
notes: Operational entity transition; verify final effective date before publication

evidence: PayPal terms eligibility language
notes: Platform access claim; not a Paxos direct redemption claim

evidence: Ripple product FAQ
notes: Issuer product page; reserve report remains stronger source for attestation

evidence: Ripple product FAQ
notes: Issuer product page; custody details should be checked against reserve reports and terms

evidence: Gemini product page reserve-asset description
notes: Issuer product page; reserve reports support monthly backing assertions

claim: Gemini's GUSD product page states Gemini customers can redeem 1 GUSD for US$1 on Gemini at any time.
page_or_section: Why GUSD? FAQ / Always redeemable for USD
evidence: Gemini product FAQ
notes: Platform-customer redemption claim; lawful-holder rights require NYDFS guidance and terms

claim: NYDFS stablecoin guidance applies only to U.S. dollar-backed stablecoins issued under DFS supervision by DFS-regulated virtual currency entities.
evidence: DFS guidance scope statement
notes: Use to avoid overgeneralizing NYDFS guidance to non-DFS issuers

claim: NYDFS guidance requires DFS-regulated U.S. dollar-backed stablecoins to be fully backed by reserve assets with market value at least equal to all outstanding units at the end of each business day.
evidence: DFS backing and redeemability requirement

claim: NYDFS guidance requires issuer redemption policies that confer on any lawful holder a right to redeem from the issuer at par in a timely fashion, with default timely redemption no later than T+2 after a compliant redemption order.
evidence: DFS redemption policy and default timing terms

claim: NYDFS guidance limits reserves to short-dated U.S. Treasury bills, overnight reverse repos collateralized by U.S. government securities, government money-market funds under DFS-approved limits, and deposit accounts at U.S. state or federally chartered depository institutions under DFS-approved restrictions.
evidence: DFS reserve asset list and custody requirements

claim: NYDFS guidance requires monthly CPA examinations of reserve assertions and an annual CPA attestation report on internal controls, structure, and procedures for compliance with reserve-attestation requirements.
evidence: DFS attestation requirements
notes: Use 'attestation' / 'examination of management assertions', not 'audit'

claim: MiCA Regulation (EU) 2023/1114 establishes uniform EU rules for crypto-asset issuers and crypto-asset service providers, including transparency and disclosure, authorization and supervision of ART/EMT issuers and CASPs, governance, holder/client protection, and market-abuse prevention.
evidence: EU Publications Office metadata and regulation scope summary
notes: Official source registered URL-only; detailed article extraction still needed

claim: Under MiCA Article 49, an e-money token issuer must redeem an e-money token at any time and at par value upon request by the holder, and redemption conditions must be stated in the crypto-asset white paper.
evidence: MiCA e-money token issuance and redeemability rule
notes: Article-level extraction from official text still needs local copy

claim: Under MiCA Article 54, funds received in exchange for e-money tokens must include at least 30% deposited in separate accounts at credit institutions, with the remainder invested in secure low-risk highly liquid assets denominated in the same official currency referenced by the token.
evidence: MiCA investment-of-funds rule for e-money tokens
notes: Article-level extraction from official text still needs local copy

claim: Federal Reserve FEDS Note says stablecoins grew by about 50% in market capitalization during 2025, with transaction volume and DeFi use also surging, while stablecoins with safer and more liquid reserve composition showed relatively stronger adoption.
evidence: Archived Fed HTML states 2025 stablecoin market-cap growth, increased transaction/DeFi use, and stronger adoption for lower-run-risk reserve compositions.
notes: Use with caution: market growth is not equivalent to payment demand.

claim: Federal Reserve FEDS Note identifies three emerging stablecoin stability vulnerabilities: complex intermediation chains, vertical integration across business functions, and accelerating retail adoption through digital wallet partnerships.
evidence: Fed introduction lists the three developments and links them to potential financial-stability vulnerabilities.
notes: Central-bank risk framing.

claim: Federal Reserve analysis says stablecoins can reduce, recycle, or restructure bank deposits depending on who demands stablecoins, what assets are converted, and how issuers allocate reserves.
evidence: Fed text states deposit impact depends on demand source, converted asset type, economic cycle, and reserve allocation.
notes: Avoid one-directional deposit-drain claims.

claim: Federal Reserve analysis says stablecoin adoption may affect bank credit through deposit-volume reductions, deposit-composition shifts, funding-cost changes, and distributional patterns; lower or more volatile deposits can constrain credit and increase liquidity-management costs.
page_or_section: Section 3: Effects on Bank Credit Provision
evidence: Fed section describes three channels and their effects on balance-sheet capacity, risk-taking, and credit provision.
notes: Useful for reserve/banking transmission chapter.

claim: Federal Reserve IFDP 1334 argues stablecoin impact on banking and credit provision varies with inflow sources and reserve composition; a two-tiered banking model can support stablecoin issuance while preserving credit creation, whereas a narrow-bank model can increase disintermediation while strengthening the fiat peg.
evidence: PDF abstract describes growth opportunities and scenarios for two-tiered banking versus narrow-bank approaches.
notes: Page-level cite verified via pdfplumber extraction of Fed_IFDP_1334 PDF page 2 abstract.

claim: IMF working paper Making Stablecoins Stable models unregulated private stablecoin issuance as suboptimal because issuers may hold risky assets to maximize profits, increasing run risk; safe-asset backing can improve stability but may reduce issuer profitability and issuance incentives without alternative revenue mechanisms.
evidence: IMF abstract describes payment-stablecoin welfare tradeoff, run risk, safe-asset backing, and issuer incentive problem.
notes: Page-level cite verified via pdfplumber extraction of IMF WP/26/74 PDF page 2 abstract.

claim: IMF working paper Stablecoins and the Future of Payments uses an event-study approach and finds that policy news supporting stablecoin payments reduced the market value of listed incumbent payment firms by 18%, about US$300bn, consistent with markets expecting stablecoins to increase payment-sector competition.
evidence: IMF abstract states event-study finding and interpretation for incumbent payment firms.
notes: Market-expectation evidence, not direct realized payment adoption. Page-level cite verified via pdfplumber extraction of IMF WP/26/52 PDF page 2 abstract.

claim: Western Union announced in 2025 that USDPT would be a U.S. Dollar Payment Token built on Solana and issued by Anchorage Digital Bank, alongside a Digital Asset Network intended to bridge digital and fiat worlds.
evidence: Archived Western Union announcement describes USDPT, Solana, Anchorage Digital Bank issuance, and Digital Asset Network.
notes: Some archived text contains mojibake but core English content is readable.

claim: Western Union announced in 2026 that USDPT launched as a U.S. dollar-denominated payment stablecoin, fully backed by U.S. dollars, issued by Anchorage Digital Bank N.A., and built on Solana for use in real-world payment systems.
evidence: Archived Western Union launch release metadata states launch, backing, issuer, chain, and payment-system framing.
notes: Needs product terms/reserve-report follow-up for legal redemption rights.

claim: Fireblocks says its platform, Dynamic embedded wallets, and TRES financial platform will support Western Union USDPT wallet, settlement, and financial-operations infrastructure, including agent settlement in USDPT and translation of onchain data into SWIFT MT940/MT942 formats.
page_or_section: Press release / The Infrastructure Powering USDPT
evidence: PRNewswire/Fireblocks release describes wallet, settlement, treasury, agent-wallet, and MT940/MT942 reporting functions.
notes: Secondary/partnership source; not core legal product terms.

claim: Artemis stablecoin metrics documentation describes a Snowflake schema for stablecoin transfer volume, transactions, supply, and address-level data across 18+ chains with None, ARTEMIS, and P2P filters.
evidence: Archived Artemis docs page metadata states schema coverage and filter categories.
notes: Methodology/data-availability claim, not a payment-volume conclusion.

claim: DeFiLlama stablecoin API snapshots provide local data for stablecoin supply and market-cap style analysis, but they should not be used as adjusted payment-volume evidence.
evidence: Archived includePrices JSON contains stablecoin supply/price fields; separate chain API supports distribution analysis.
notes: Interpretive guardrail based on API contents and project rule against equating supply/raw data with payments.

claim: Paxos stablecoin terms state Paxos can freeze and upgrade USD Stablecoins on an aggregate basis, can freeze access to USD Stablecoins or backing dollars pursuant to legal directive or other listed circumstances, and may refuse redemption when stablecoins are used for illegal or sanctioned activity.
evidence: Archived Paxos terms include upgrade/freeze provisions, legal-directive freeze language, seizure/forfeiture language, and illegal/sanctioned activity redemption restriction.
notes: Applies to USD Stablecoins as defined by Paxos terms; map exact token coverage during issuer-specific extraction.

claim: Ripple RLUSD user terms say prohibited uses can lead Ripple to suspend or terminate user access to RLUSD, potentially making the user unable to use RLUSD, and prohibited businesses can lead to inability to redeem RLUSD for U.S. dollars with Ripple.
evidence: Archived Ripple terms include Prohibited Uses and Prohibited Businesses sections with suspension/termination and redemption-consequence language.
notes: Terms are user-facing and should be reconciled with NYDFS lawful-holder redemption guidance.

claim: Circle USDC terms state that users may issue USDC for USD and redeem USDC for USD directly with Circle only through a Circle Mint account in good standing; transfer of USDC assigns a holder the right to redeem only if that holder is eligible for and registers a Circle Mint account.
page_or_section: USDC Terms Section 2 Scope of USDC and Key Terms
evidence: Archived Circle USDC terms describe USDC Services, Circle Mint account requirement, holder assignment, ineligible holder exclusion, and Circle's 1 USDC for 1 USD redemption commitment subject to terms, law, and fees.
notes: Clarifies direct redeemer scope; use alongside transparency-page reserve claim.

claim: Circle USDC terms state that USDC holders are not entitled to interest or returns earned on USDC reserves, and USDC itself does not generate interest or return for holders.
page_or_section: USDC Terms Section 2 Scope of USDC and Key Terms
evidence: Archived Circle USDC terms state that reserve interest or returns belong outside holder entitlement and USDC only represents the right to redeem through a Circle account.
notes: Useful for distinguishing reserve income from token-holder yield.

claim: Tether token terms state that a user must be a verified customer of Tether to cause Tether Tokens to be issued or redeemed by Tether, and that the right to purchase or redeem Tether Tokens is a personal contractual right.
page_or_section: About Tether Tokens: Issuances and Redemptions
evidence: Archived Tether terms describe redemption payment to a designated bank account, verified-customer requirement, personal contractual right, fees, and no FDIC/SIPC-style protection.
notes: Clarifies direct redemption eligibility; not a broad lawful-holder right.

claim: Tether token terms define Prohibited Persons to include U.S. Persons except certain Eligible Contract Participants accepted by Tether in its discretion, Canadian Persons, Singaporean Persons, sanctioned persons, and persons in prohibited jurisdictions.
evidence: Archived Tether terms define prohibited persons and jurisdictions including U.S. persons with a limited discretionary ECP exception.
notes: Use for jurisdiction/user eligibility comparison.

claim: FDUSD/FDD terms state that holders may sell FDD to FD121 only if they are eligible for and register a FD121 Account; holders who are not eligible or fail to register are not entitled to sell FDD to FD121.
page_or_section: Scope of FDD and Key Terms
evidence: Archived FDD terms describe FD121 Account requirement, transfer of rights to subsequent holders, exclusion of non-eligible/non-registered holders, and conditional 1 FDD for 1 Dollar sale mechanics.
notes: Clarifies FDUSD direct redemption/sale scope.

claim: FDUSD/FDD terms allow FD121, subject to applicable rules, to cancel, suspend, restrict, defer, or limit buying and selling of FDD in circumstances such as reserve realization problems, inability to meet sale requests, significant sale requests, market volatility, exchange disruption, emergency conditions, or legal requirements.
page_or_section: Scope of FDD and Key Terms
evidence: Archived FDD terms list circumstances in which FD121 may limit or suspend buying and selling between FD121 and FD121 Account users.
notes: Important holder-protection and liquidity-stress caveat.

claim: FD121 account terms exclude individuals or entities in the United States from FD121 Account eligibility and allow FD121 to terminate the agreement or transactions if representations become untrue or misleading.
evidence: Archived FD121 account agreement states users must not be acting for a U.S. individual or entity and permits immediate termination for misrepresentation.
notes: Use with FDUSD redemption-scope claims; check jurisdiction updates before final publication.

claim: Ethena USDe terms distinguish Mint Users from Holding Users: only whitelisted Mint Users that complete KYC/AML and onboarding may create or redeem USDe directly with Ethena BVI, while Holding Users are not Ethena BVI customers and do not have a right to redeem USDe with Ethena BVI.
evidence: Archived Ethena terms define Mint Users and Holding Users and state Holding Users may not redeem unless onboarded and whitelisted as Mint Users.
notes: Clarifies that secondary-market USDe holding is not equivalent to direct issuer redemption.

claim: Ethena USDe terms state that users in the United States are not eligible to become Mint Users, and USDe itself does not generate interest or return for holders even if reserves are held in yield-generating instruments.
evidence: Archived Ethena terms state U.S. user Mint User ineligibility, no holder entitlement to reserve returns, and no interest or return generated by USDe itself.
notes: Separate USDe from sUSDe and from fiat-backed payment stablecoins.

claim: Ethena's USDe overview describes USDe as a delta-neutral synthetic dollar backed within the crypto ecosystem, where approved KYC/KYB parties from permitted jurisdictions can mint and redeem directly after whitelisting while other users can acquire USDe through external liquidity pools.
evidence: Archived Ethena overview describes delta-neutral synthetic dollar design, backing/margin collateral, whitelisted mint/redeem access, and external liquidity pool acquisition.
notes: Product overview claim; reconcile with terms and risk disclosures.

claim: Paxos EU redemption page states that retail USDG redemptions through Paxos require EU residency, USDG on Ethereum or Solana, an EU-based bank account, and accurate KYC/AML information; users who do not meet the requirements cannot submit the redemption request.
evidence: Archived Paxos EU redemptions page lists partner conversion options and Paxos retail redemption eligibility criteria.
notes: Specific to USDG EU redemption route; not a general PYUSD/USDP right.

claim: MiCA Article 36 requires issuers of asset-referenced tokens to maintain a reserve of assets that covers referenced-asset risks and addresses liquidity risks from holders' permanent redemption rights; the reserve must be legally and operationally segregated and at least equal to aggregate holder claims.
evidence: ESMA interactive MiCA text for Article 36 states reserve maintenance, risk/liquidity coverage, legal and operational segregation, reserve valuation, and at-least-equal aggregate value requirements; official PDF is archived locally as MICA_004.
notes: Use as ART reserve claim; ESMA interactive page used for readable extraction because EUR-Lex HTML fetch was challenge-blocked.

claim: MiCA Article 39 gives holders of asset-referenced tokens a permanent right of redemption against the issuer and, where the issuer cannot meet obligations under Title III Chapter 6, in respect of reserve assets; redemption must be available in funds or referenced assets and must not be subject to a fee except as provided under Article 46.
evidence: ESMA interactive MiCA text for Article 39 states the permanent redemption right, redemption in funds or referenced assets, required redemption policy elements, stressed-market procedures, and no-fee rule.
notes: Separates ART redemption from EMT at-par redemption.

claim: MiCA Article 40 prohibits issuers of asset-referenced tokens and crypto-asset service providers from granting interest in relation to asset-referenced tokens, treating remuneration or benefits linked to holding time as interest.
evidence: ESMA interactive MiCA text for Article 40 states the prohibition of interest and broad treatment of holding-time-linked remuneration or equivalent benefits.
notes: Use for ART yield/interest comparison; separate from issuer terms.

claim: The unified Paxos USD Stablecoin Agreement allocates issuance among three Paxos entities: Paxos Trust Company, N.A. issues USDP, PYUSD and BUSD (redemption-only); Paxos Digital Singapore Pte. Ltd. and Paxos Issuance Europe Oy (PIE) jointly issue USDG.
evidence: Paxos terms text identifies USDP, PYUSD and BUSD as Paxos Trust issued and USDG as jointly issued by Paxos Digital and PIE; Section 3.1.1 confirms Paxos Trust issues USDP/PYUSD/BUSD and Paxos Digital/PIE jointly issue USDG.
notes: Use this to separate issuer entity from product across the Paxos family; replaces vague "Paxos" attribution.

claim: Only Paxos Customers may purchase or redeem Paxos USD Stablecoins (USDP, PYUSD, USDG) directly with Paxos; Non-Customer Token Holders defined in the agreement do not have a direct purchase or redemption right with Paxos.
evidence: Paxos terms define Non-Customer Token Holder as "anyone that is not a Customer ... that does, or intends to, hold USD Stablecoins"; Section 2.2.2 states "Only Customers may purchase USD Stablecoins from us or redeem USD Stablecoins from us"; Section 2.7.1 conditions redemption on having an Account.
notes: Direct-redemption gating for PYUSD and USDP; complements existing USDC (CLAIM_058), USDT (CLAIM_060), FDUSD (CLAIM_062), USDe (CLAIM_065) and USDG EU route (CLAIM_068).

claim: Paxos USD Stablecoins are explicitly not designed to create returns, profits, increase in value, or otherwise accrue financial benefit to Customer or Non-Customer Token Holders.
evidence: Paxos terms state "Our USD Stablecoins are not designed to create returns or profits for holders, increase in value, or otherwise accrue financial benefit to the Customer Token Holders and Non-Customer Token Holders."
notes: Paxos-family equivalent of USDC no-holder-yield (CLAIM_059) and USDe no-holder-yield (CLAIM_066); applies to PYUSD, USDP, USDG.

claim: Paxos Trust USD Stablecoin reserves are restricted to three forms: (i) fiat currency held in FDIC-insured bank accounts; (ii) direct or repo investments in debt instruments expressly guaranteed by the full faith and credit of the U.S. Government and/or money-market funds composed of such instruments; and (iii) fiat currency held at FDIC-insured banks in excess of FDIC limits when Paxos reasonably believes funds must remain liquid; Paxos Digital and PIE follow analogous restrictions under MAS and Finnish FIN-FSA frameworks.
evidence: Paxos terms enumerate the permitted reserve forms for Paxos Trust, Paxos Digital and PIE, with cash management restricted to bank deposits and U.S. Treasury debt instruments / repos / money-market funds composed of such instruments.
notes: Reserve composition for PYUSD, USDP and USDG; supports reserve-assets chapter without invoking attestation language.

claim: Paxos has a contractual and technical right to freeze and upgrade all USD Stablecoin tokens (on an aggregate basis) regardless of where they are held; this freeze and upgrade right applies to all holders including Non-Customer Token Holders.
evidence: Paxos terms state "Paxos has the ability and the right to freeze and upgrade all USD Stablecoins tokens (on an aggregate basis), regardless of where the USD Stablecoins are being held" and "This Section 3.4 applies to all holders of USD Stablecoins, regardless of whether the holder is a Customer of Paxos."
notes: Tightens CLAIM_056 by specifying the all-holders scope of the freeze/upgrade right for PYUSD, USDP and USDG.

claim: Only Gemini Customers may exchange U.S. dollars for Gemini Dollars or Gemini Dollars for U.S. dollars at Gemini; for non-Gemini-Customers, obtaining or using GUSD does not create or represent any relationship between the holder and Gemini and does not subject Gemini to any obligations toward that holder.
page_or_section: "Using the Gemini Dollar" subsection of Gemini Dollar section
evidence: Gemini Trust User Agreement states "only Gemini Customers may exchange U.S. dollars for Gemini Dollars and/or Gemini Dollars for U.S. dollars at Gemini" and that for non-Customers "obtaining or using the Gemini Dollar does not create or represent any relationship between you and us ... and does not subject us ... to any obligations whatsoever as they relate to you."
notes: Direct-redemption gating for GUSD; distinguishes Gemini-customer redemption from broader lawful-holder rights.

claim: Gemini Dollar reserves are held across three account types: (i) U.S. dollar omnibus accounts at U.S. FDIC-insured banks, (ii) money market accounts holding money market funds invested in securities issued or guaranteed by the United States or certain U.S. government agencies or instrumentalities, and (iii) Treasury accounts holding U.S. Treasury Obligations; Gemini manages this reserve to a 1:1 ratio with outstanding Gemini Dollars.
evidence: Gemini Trust User Agreement defines Gemini Dollar Omnibus Accounts, Gemini Dollar Money Market Accounts and Gemini Dollar Treasury Accounts and states "Gemini manages the Gemini Dollar Reserve to maintain a 1:1 ratio of Gemini Dollars to the U.S. dollar value of the Gemini Dollar Reserve."
notes: GUSD reserve composition; refines CLAIM_034 by specifying the three discrete account categories.

claim: Gemini's contractual redemption commitment for GUSD Customer sell Orders is "timely", explicitly defined as not more than one full Business Day after the Business Day on which Gemini receives the sell Order.
evidence: Gemini Trust User Agreement defines "Timely" redemption as "a redemption of not more than one full Business Day after the Business Day on which Gemini receives a sell Order for the relevant Gemini Dollars."
notes: Concrete redemption-timing commitment for GUSD; useful for issuer-comparison redemption speed analysis.

claim: As of February 28, 2025, USDP attestation reports are issued monthly by KPMG LLP as Paxos's independent third-party accounting firm and are conducted in accordance with attestation standards established by the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA); reports issued prior to February 28, 2025 were by WithumSmith+Brown, PC under the same AICPA standards.
evidence: Paxos USDP transparency page states "Attestation reports posted on or after February 28, 2025 are issued by KPMG LLP, an independent third-party accounting firm. KPMG LLP's examination is conducted in accordance with attestation standards established by the American Institute of Certified Public Accountants (AICPA)."
notes: Specifies USDP examination provider and AICPA attestation framing; this is an examination/attestation, not a financial-statement audit.

claim: Paxos has announced on the USDP transparency page that it no longer proactively provides monthly reserve composition reports for USDP, separately from the KPMG attestation reports.
evidence: Paxos USDP transparency page footnote states "Paxos no longer proactively provides monthly reserve reports for USDP. Questions? Reach out to press@paxos.com."
notes: Explains the long-standing absence of a USDP monthly reserve composition PDF in the archive; the report is not missing but has been discontinued by the issuer.

claim: GENIUS Act Section 3(a) makes it unlawful for any person other than a permitted payment stablecoin issuer to issue a payment stablecoin in the United States; the Secretary of the Treasury may issue limited safe harbors that are consistent with the Act, limited in scope, and apply only to a de minimis volume of transactions, or in determined unusual and exigent circumstances.
evidence: GENIUS Act Public Law 119-27 Section 3(a) states "It shall be unlawful for any person other than a permitted payment stablecoin issuer to issue a payment stablecoin in the United States"; Section 3(c) authorises Treasury limited safe harbors meeting the three criteria, with separate unusual-and-exigent-circumstances safe harbor.
notes: Core issuer-eligibility provision of the GENIUS Act; supports chapter 4 framing of who may legally issue payment stablecoins in the US.

claim: GENIUS Act Section 3(b) imposes a 3-year transition after enactment after which it is unlawful for any digital asset service provider to offer or sell a payment stablecoin to a person in the United States unless the stablecoin is issued by a permitted payment stablecoin issuer; foreign payment stablecoin issuers may have their tokens offered or sold by a digital asset service provider in the US only if the foreign issuer has the technological capability to comply, and will comply, with any lawful order and any reciprocal arrangement under Section 18.
evidence: GENIUS Act Section 3(b)(1) sets the 3-year transition cutoff; Section 3(b)(2) conditions foreign issuer access on the technological capability and commitment to comply with lawful orders and Section 18 reciprocal arrangements.
notes: Combines transition timing and foreign-issuer treatment; supports both chapter 4 (law) and chapter 6 (USDPT framing as a permitted US issuer rather than a foreign import).

claim: GENIUS Act Section 4(1)(A) restricts permitted payment stablecoin reserves to an exhaustive list: U.S. coins and currency or money in a Federal Reserve Bank account; demand deposits at insured depository institutions (subject to safety-and-soundness limits); Treasury bills, notes, or bonds with remaining or original maturity of 93 days or less; overnight repurchase agreements backed by such Treasuries; overnight reverse repurchase agreements collateralized by such Treasuries with overcollateralization that are tri-party, centrally cleared, or bilateral with adequately creditworthy counterparties; registered investment-company or government money market funds invested solely in the foregoing assets; and any other similarly liquid Federal Government-issued asset approved by the primary Federal payment stablecoin regulator.
evidence: GENIUS Act Section 4(1)(A) enumerates the eligible reserve asset categories; the statute caps Treasury maturity at 93 days and requires repos/reverse repos to be overnight with overcollateralization for reverse repos, plus a primary-regulator approval gateway for any additional similar liquid asset.
notes: Authoritative reserve-composition rule; supports chapter 3 (reserve assets) reserve-comparison framing and chapter 4 (law) US baseline.

claim: GENIUS Act Sections 4(1)(C)-(D) and 4(3) impose monthly disclosure and examination obligations on permitted payment stablecoin issuers: monthly publication on the issuer's website of the number of outstanding payment stablecoins, the amount and composition of reserves (including average tenor and geographic custody of each reserve category), examination of the previous month-end report by a registered public accounting firm, and CEO/CFO certification subject to criminal penalties for knowingly false certification.
evidence: GENIUS Act Section 4(1)(C) requires monthly reserve composition publication including tenor and custody geography; Section 4(3)(A) requires monthly examination by a registered public accounting firm; Section 4(3)(C) imposes criminal-penalty liability for knowingly false CEO/CFO certifications equivalent to 18 U.S.C. 1350(c).
notes: Establishes the federal monthly-attestation regime; complements existing AICPA-attestation-vs-audit framing in issuer claims.

claim: GENIUS Act Section 4(11) prohibits any permitted payment stablecoin issuer or foreign payment stablecoin issuer from paying the holder of a payment stablecoin any form of interest or yield (whether in cash, tokens, or other consideration) solely in connection with the holding, use, or retention of the payment stablecoin.
evidence: GENIUS Act Section 4(11) states "No permitted payment stablecoin issuer or foreign payment stablecoin issuer shall pay the holder of any payment stablecoin any form of interest or yield (whether in cash, tokens, or other consideration) solely in connection with the holding, use, or retention of such payment stablecoin."
notes: Federal statutory equivalent of MiCA ART Article 40 (CLAIM_071) and Paxos/Circle no-holder-yield issuer terms (CLAIM_059, CLAIM_066, CLAIM_074); use for cross-jurisdiction yield comparison.

claim: GENIUS Act Section 11 establishes that in any insolvency proceeding of a permitted payment stablecoin issuer under Federal or State law, the claim of a customer against the issuer with respect to payment stablecoins held by the issuer for that customer shall have priority over the claims of any person other than another customer with respect to payment stablecoins held by the issuer.
evidence: GENIUS Act Section 11 "CUSTOMER PRIORITY" provision states customer claims with respect to payment stablecoins held by an issuer take priority over any non-customer claims in any insolvency proceeding, subject only to other customers' claims with respect to the same stablecoins.
notes: Holder-protection baseline; closes the long-standing chapter-4 question on US insolvency treatment of stablecoin holders.

claim: The Bank of England's 2023 discussion paper proposes that issuers of stablecoins used in systemic payment systems should back stablecoins fully (100%) with central bank deposits; this preferred backing model is intended to eliminate credit, liquidity and market risk in backing assets and to support singleness of money (par-value exchange with other forms of sterling-denominated money).
evidence: BoE 2023 discussion paper states the Bank judges that "the 100% central bank deposits model is most appropriate for systemic payment systems using stablecoins operating in the UK"; full backing with central bank deposits "means that the credit, liquidity or market risks associated with other choices of backing assets are eliminated".
notes: UK systemic stablecoin baseline as of 2023; superseded for the backing-asset rule by BOE_004 (CLAIM_090) but the singleness-of-money framing remains the policy anchor.

claim: The Bank of England's 2023 discussion paper requires issuers of systemic-payment-system stablecoins to maintain a recovery and administration plan to fulfil redemptions in the event of failure of any firm in the payment chain, and to hold a shortfall reserve of assets on statutory trust for the benefit of coinholders in addition to capital requirements under the PFMI approach.
page_or_section: Section 4 (recovery/administration plan, p. 64) and Section 5 (shortfall reserve, p. 71)
evidence: BoE 2023 paper states issuers "would further be required to maintain a recovery and administration plan to be able to fulfil redemptions in the case of a failure of any firm in the payment chain" (p. 64), and that issuers "would need to hold a reserve of assets to mitigate these shortfall risks ... held by issuers on statutory trust for the benefit of coinholders" (p. 71).
notes: Holder-protection rule for UK systemic regime; analogous to MiCA ART recovery/redemption plans (CLAIM_095, CLAIM_096) but with the statutory trust + shortfall reserve construct unique to the BoE proposal.

claim: The Bank of England's 2025 consultation revises the 2023 backing-asset proposal: at least 40% of backing assets must be held as unremunerated central bank deposits at the Bank of England, up to 60% may be held in short-term sterling-denominated UK government debt securities, and a step-up regime allows systemically important issuers at launch to hold up to 95% of backing assets in UK government debt securities as they scale (reducing to 60% once the stablecoin reaches a scale appropriate to mitigate systemic risks).
evidence: BoE 2025 consultation states "At least 40% of backing assets to be held as deposits at the Bank ... Up to 60% of backing assets to be held in short-term sterling-denominated UK government debt securities" and "Stablecoin issuers recognised by HMT as systemic at launch ... could be allowed to hold up to 95% of their backing assets in sterling-denominated UK government debt securities as they scale."
notes: Updates CLAIM_088 backing-asset rule for v0.3 chapter 4. The unremunerated CBD component remains the policy lever; the 60% UK gilt allowance is the concession to business-model viability raised in the 2023 consultation responses.

claim: The Bank of England's 2025 consultation proposes per-coin holding limits for systemic sterling-denominated stablecoins: £20,000 for individuals (cross-referencing the digital pound consultation range of £10,000-£20,000) and £10 million for businesses, with potential exemptions for retail businesses and intermediaries servicing retail customers that may need to hold large balances.
page_or_section: Section 1: Introduction (holding limits table) and proposed limits
evidence: BoE 2025 consultation states "Holdings by individuals would be subject to the proposed per-coin limits of £20,000. Retail businesses ... could be exempted from the proposed £10 million business limits" and frames the £20,000 individual limit against the digital pound consultation range of £10,000-£20,000.
notes: Distinct UK feature with no GENIUS or MiCA analogue; introduces a quantitative cap on stablecoin holdings that should be flagged in chapter 4 cross-jurisdiction comparison.

claim: ESMA's January 2025 supervisory briefing for the authorisation of crypto-asset service providers (CASPs) under MiCA explicitly states that there are no low-risk CASPs and that CASPs above thresholds of 1,000,000 yearly active users in the EU or a €3,000,000,000 balance sheet, more than 200,000 yearly active users outside the home Member State, complex group structures spanning multiple regulatory frameworks (EMI/MiFID/CASP), or outsourcing of key functions or outsourcing outside the EU should be subjected to an elevated level of supervisory scrutiny by national competent authorities.
page_or_section: Section 3.1 Core principles and Section 3.2 Key elements that create a higher risk, pp. 6-8
evidence: ESMA briefing states "there are no low-risk CASPs" (p. 6); "CASPs with more than 1.000.000 yearly active users in the EU or a balance sheet size of €3.000.000.000 should be subjected to an elevated level of scrutiny" (p. 7); "CASPs with more than 200.000 yearly active users outside the home Member State should be subjected to an elevated level of scrutiny" (p. 7).
notes: Supervisory expectation rather than primary statute; useful for chapter 4 EU CASP / market-infrastructure framing and for understanding how stablecoin distribution platforms will be supervised in the EU.

claim: ESMA's February 2025 Guidelines on transfer services for crypto-assets under MiCA Article 82 require CASPs providing transfer services on behalf of clients to provide pre-contractual disclosure including the supported DLT networks, maximum execution time, reasonably estimated time or number of block confirmations for irreversibility, charges and fees, conditions for rejection of an instruction, and liability for unauthorised or incorrectly executed transfers; in addition, transfer initiation or execution may not take place before steps have been taken to ensure compliance with the Travel Rule equivalent in Regulation (EU) 2023/1113 (TOFR), including Article 14.
page_or_section: Section 5.1 (general policies and procedures), Section 5.2 (information on individual transfers), and Section 5.5 (liability), pp. 6-10
evidence: ESMA Guidelines state that CASPs must provide pre-contractual information including "the maximum execution time for the transfer of crypto-assets service to be provided" and "for each DLT network, reasonably estimated time or number of block confirmations needed for the transfer to be irreversible" (Guideline 1); "initiation or execution of the transfer does not take place before adequate steps have been taken to ensure compliance with TOFR, including Article 14 thereof" (paragraph 18).
notes: Operational rules for stablecoin transfers within the EU; relevant to chapter 6 (cross-border payments) and the EU leg of any USDPT/Anchorage distribution.

claim: MiCA Article 45 imposes specific additional obligations on issuers of significant asset-referenced tokens: a remuneration policy that does not create incentives to relax risk standards, the ability for tokens to be held in custody by different CASPs on a fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory (FRAND) basis, and a liquidity management policy and procedures ensuring the reserve assets have a resilient liquidity profile under stress; EBA regulatory technical standards specify the minimum content of the liquidity policy, including a minimum amount of deposits in each referenced official currency which cannot be lower than 60% of the amount referenced in each official currency.
evidence: MiCA Article 45 states issuers of significant ART must "adopt, implement and maintain a remuneration policy that promotes the sound and effective risk management", that significant ART "can be held in custody by different crypto-asset service providers ... on a fair, reasonable and non-discriminatory basis", and that EBA RTS specify "minimum amount of deposits in each official currency referenced, which cannot be lower than 60% of the amount referenced in each official currency".
notes: Significant-ART additional obligations layer over CLAIM_069 (Article 36 reserve) and CLAIM_070 (Article 39 redemption); the 60% deposit-in-referenced-currency floor is the key liquidity rule for stablecoins that EBA classifies as significant.

claim: MiCA Article 46 (Recovery plan) requires every issuer of an asset-referenced token (significant or not) to draw up and maintain a recovery plan with measures to restore compliance with the reserve-of-assets requirements; recovery options must include liquidity fees on redemptions, limits on the amount of asset-referenced tokens that can be redeemed on any working day, and suspension of redemptions; the competent authority may require the issuer to implement the plan and may temporarily suspend the redemption of asset-referenced tokens where justified by holder interests and financial stability.
evidence: MiCA Article 46 states the recovery plan "shall include appropriate conditions and procedures to ensure the timely implementation of recovery actions as well as a wide range of recovery options, including: (a) liquidity fees on redemptions; (b) limits on the amount of the asset-referenced token that can be redeemed on any working day; (c) suspension of redemptions".
notes: Recovery plan rule for MiCA ART, parallel to BoE recovery/administration plan (CLAIM_089). Note that suspension of redemptions is explicitly contemplated, even though Article 39 establishes a "permanent redemption right".

claim: MiCA Article 47 (Redemption plan) requires every issuer of an asset-referenced token to draw up and maintain an operational plan to support orderly redemption of each ART, to be implemented upon a competent authority decision that the issuer is unable or likely to be unable to fulfil its obligations, including in cases of insolvency, resolution, or withdrawal of authorisation; the plan must demonstrate ability to redeem outstanding ART without undue economic harm to holders or market stability and must include contractual arrangements, procedures, systems including a temporary administrator designation, and continuity of critical activities.
evidence: MiCA Article 47 states the redemption plan "shall demonstrate the ability of the issuer of the asset-referenced token to carry out the redemption of the outstanding asset-referenced token issued without causing undue economic harm to its holders or to the stability of the markets of the reserve assets" and that it "shall include contractual arrangements, procedures and systems, including the designation of a temporary administrator in accordance with applicable law, to ensure the equitable treatment of all holders".
notes: Wind-down rule for MiCA ART; complements GENIUS Act Section 11 customer-priority insolvency rule (CLAIM_087) but with an EU-style operational plan + temporary administrator construct rather than a US bankruptcy-priority rule.

claim: MiCA Articles 56-58 establish a parallel significant-token regime for e-money tokens: EBA classifies EMTs as significant where at least three of the Article 43(1) criteria are met, supervisory responsibility transfers from the home Member State competent authority to EBA, and significant EMT issuers are subject to additional obligations including independent audits every six months (Article 58(1) by derogation from Article 36(9)).
evidence: MiCA Article 56(1) requires EBA classification of EMTs where "at least three of the criteria set out in Article 43(1) are met"; Article 58(1) imposes Articles 36, 37, 38 and Article 45(1)-(4) on EMI issuers of significant EMTs and requires that "the independent audit shall, in respect of issuers of significant e-money tokens, be mandated every six months".
notes: Closes the long-standing chapter-4 ART/EMT significant-token gap. Note that EMT issuers must already be authorised credit institutions or e-money institutions under Article 48; the significant-EMT layer adds Article 45-style obligations and the six-monthly independent audit cadence.

claim: The MakerDAO Multi-Collateral Dai (MCD) Protocol generates Dai by allowing users to deposit collateral assets approved by Maker Governance into Maker Vaults; every Dai in circulation is directly backed by excess collateral (collateral value greater than the value of the Dai debt); MKR holders govern the protocol by voting on the set of accepted collateral types and risk parameters including Stability Fees and Liquidation Ratios.
page_or_section: About the Maker Protocol / Collateral Assets sections, pp. 2-6
evidence: MakerDAO MCD whitepaper states "Dai is a decentralized, unbiased, collateral-backed cryptocurrency soft-pegged to the US Dollar", "Every Dai in circulation is directly backed by excess collateral", "MKR holders manage the Maker Protocol ... through Executive Voting and Governance Polling" and "MKR holders ... vote on corresponding Risk Parameters for each collateral asset"; Vaults are funded with a specific type and amount of collateral approved by Maker Governance.
notes: Core DAI / Maker mechanics; supports chapter 2 placement of DAI/USDS in the crypto/RWA-collateralised category, not fiat-backed.

claim: In the MakerDAO MCD Protocol, any Maker Vault that falls below the governance-set Liquidation Ratio is automatically liquidated through Maker Protocol Collateral Auctions; if a Collateral Auction does not raise enough Dai to cover the Vault's outstanding obligation plus the Liquidation Penalty, the deficit is converted into Protocol Surplus or covered by MKR dilution; if the auction raises more than needed, the excess converts to a Reverse Collateral Auction that returns leftover collateral to the original Vault owner.
page_or_section: Liquidation of Risky Maker Vaults section, pp. 8-9
evidence: MakerDAO MCD whitepaper describes liquidation mechanics: "any Maker Vault deemed too risky (according to parameters established by Maker Governance) is liquidated through automated Maker Protocol auctions", and Collateral Auctions convert to Reverse Collateral Auctions when the auction raises enough to cover obligations plus Liquidation Penalty; "If the Collateral Auction does not raise enough Dai to cover the Vault's outstanding obligation, the deficit is converted into Protocol" Surplus / MKR dilution.
notes: Liquidation / governance backstop mechanism critical for understanding the difference between fiat-backed redemption rights and crypto-collateral liquidation; supports chapter 2 risk framing and chapter 8 failure-case analysis.

claim: The Dai Savings Rate (DSR) is a global system parameter in the MakerDAO MCD Protocol that allows any Dai holder to earn savings automatically by locking Dai into the DSR contract; there is no minimum deposit and users can withdraw any or all of their Dai from the DSR contract at any time; the equivalent feature under the rebranded Sky Protocol is the Sky Savings Rate paid through sUSDS.
page_or_section: The Dai Savings Rate section, p. 12
evidence: MakerDAO MCD whitepaper states "The Dai Savings Rate (DSR) allows any Dai holder to earn savings automatically and natively by locking their Dai into the DSR contract in the Maker Protocol" and "Users aren't required to deposit a minimum amount to earn the DSR, and they can withdraw any or all of their Dai from the DSR contract at any time".
notes: DSR / Sky Savings Rate is the savings/yield module distinguishing DAI/USDS from fiat-backed payment stablecoins; complements CLAIM_102 on the Sky rebrand and sUSDS.

claim: The MakerDAO MCD Protocol relies on three classes of external actors to maintain operations: Keepers (independent, usually automated market actors incentivised by economic opportunities in the protocol), Oracles (external feeds with special permissions assigned by MKR voters that supply price information), and Global Settlers / Emergency Oracles (external actors authorised by MKR voters with the unilateral authority to trigger an Emergency Shutdown of the protocol).
evidence: MakerDAO MCD whitepaper states "the Maker Protocol involves groups of external actors to maintain operations: Keepers, Oracles, and Global Settlers (Emergency Oracles)"; Keepers are "incentivized by ... economic incentives presented by the Protocol"; Oracles and Global Settlers are "external actors with special permissions in the system assigned to them by MKR voters"; Global Settlers "have the authority to unilaterally trigger an Emergency Shutdown".
notes: Oracle and governance-actor surface area; supports chapter 2 risk framing that DAI/USDS depends on off-protocol actors and is not solely a smart-contract artifact.

claim: The Sky.money product page (Sky Protocol's post-MakerDAO rebrand) describes USDS as the rebranded stablecoin with sUSDS as a yield-bearing wrapper accruing the Sky Savings Rate, stUSDS as a SKY-backed-lending risk capital token, and SKY as the governance token; the product page advertises 1:1 USDC-to-USDS conversion in both directions with zero fees or slippage; sUSDS, stUSDS, and Sky Ecosystem Rewards are flagged as "Currently unavailable in the US".
evidence: Sky.money product page describes "Get non-custodial access to market-leading returns on stablecoins through a suite of savings tools from Sky Protocol", "sUSDS yield is generated through the Sky Savings Rate, which is backed by the Sky Agent Network and governed via Sky Protocol", "1:1 USDC to USDS conversion. Both ways. Swap anytime with zero fees or slippage", and "Currently unavailable in the US" notices on sUSDS, stUSDS, and Ecosystem Rewards entry points.
notes: Captures the MakerDAO → Sky rebrand at the user-facing layer and the PSM-style USDC↔USDS conversion; flags the US-jurisdiction unavailability of the yield-bearing modules.

claim: Ethena's USDe is a synthetic-dollar stablecoin whose peg is maintained by delta-neutral hedging: when a whitelisted user provides backing assets such as USDT to mint USDe, Ethena opens a corresponding short perpetual futures position on a derivatives exchange of approximately the same notional value; this allows USDe to maintain only 1:1 collateralisation (not overcollateralisation) because the price-change risk of the backing asset is offset by an equal-and-opposite change in the value of the short perpetual hedge.
page_or_section: How USDe Works page / Delta Neutrality section
evidence: Ethena documentation states "USDe derives its relative peg stability from executing automated and programmatic delta-neutral hedges with respect to the underlying backing assets" and "Since the backing assets can be perfectly hedged with a short position of equivalent notional, USDe only requires 1:1 'collateralization.'".
notes: Core distinction between USDe (synthetic-dollar via perp hedge) and fiat-backed payment stablecoins; supports chapter 2 placement of USDe in the synthetic-dollar category, not fiat-backed.

claim: Ethena documentation states that USDe backing assets are held in Off-Exchange Settlement institutional-grade custody solutions and that Ethena delegates but never transfers custody of backing assets to centralised derivatives exchanges; this Off-Exchange Custody arrangement is intended to allow Ethena to delegate and undelegate backing assets between custodian and exchange to settle funding or realised P&L without being exposed to exchange-specific idiosyncratic counterparty risk.
page_or_section: Off Exchange Custody section / Backing Asset Custody page
evidence: Ethena documentation states "Assets backing USDe remain in 'Off-Exchange Settlement' institutional grade solutions at all times. The only time collateral flows between custody and exchange is to settle funding or realized P&L" and "This enables Ethena to delegate/undelegate backing assets to centralized exchanges without being exposed to exchange-specific idiosyncratic risk".
notes: Counterparty-risk mitigation architecture; should NOT be read as eliminating exchange risk altogether; USDe holders remain exposed to off-exchange custodian default and to derivative-exchange operational disruption affecting hedge execution.

claim: Ethena publishes monthly custodian attestations validating the existence, control, and value of the USDe backing assets; the published attestation series runs at least from April 2024 through April 2026; Ethena states that none of the backing assets of USDe reside directly on any of its exchange partners.
evidence: Ethena custodian attestations resource page states "Monthly attestations are completed with the custodians to validate the existence, control, and value of the backing assets of USDe" and "These attestations continue to demonstrate that NONE of the backing assets of USDe reside directly on any of our exchange partners"; the page lists monthly attestation links from at least April 2024 through April 2026.
notes: Custodian-level attestation series, distinct from a financial-statement audit; does not substitute for assurance over the hedging system or the derivatives-exchange leg.

claim: Ethena's Reserve Fund is described as an additional margin of safety behind USDe to fund periods of negative perpetual-funding-rate payments and to act as a bidder of last resort for USDe in open markets; the fund's composition is stablecoin assets (USDC and USDT) for USD-margined contracts plus a smaller ETH allocation for ETH-margined contracts, with other assets as determined by governance; the fund is controlled by a 4/10 multi-sig with keys held by Ethena Labs contributors, capitalised from a portion of protocol revenue and private placement investors, with a stated size of US$46.6 million as of Q4 2024.
evidence: Ethena Reserve Fund documentation describes the fund as an "additional margin of safety behind USDe to provide a source of capital to pay for periods of negative funding, as well as a bidder of last resort for USDe in open markets"; composition lists "Stablecoin assets including USDC and USDT for USD-M contracts" and "Smaller ETH backing assets allocation for ETH-M contracts"; security is "Controlled by 4/10 multi-sig with keys held by contributors sitting within Ethena Labs"; "the Reserve Fund size is $46.6m as of Q4 2024".
notes: Reserve fund size is a point-in-time snapshot from Q4 2024 and may be stale; the bidder-of-last-resort role depends on continued protocol revenue and discretionary governance allocations.
